title: Spin-$s$ model with competing interactions on diamond-decorated lattices
authors: D. V. Dmitriev, V. Ya. Krivnov, O. A. Vasilyev
abstract: We investigate the ground state properties, magnetization, and low-temperature thermodynamics of the ferromagnetic-antiferromagnetic spin-$s$ model on diamond-decorated lattices with ideal diamond units, incorporating bilinear Heisenberg and higher-order exchange interactions between diagonal spins-$σ$. Local conservation of the composite spin on each diamond diagonal enables exact analysis. For the pure Heisenberg case, the system undergoes a series of $2σ$ transitions between monomer-dimer (MD), ferrimagnetic (Ferri) and ferromagnetic (F) phases with different optimal composite spin values as the coupling ratio varies. In the presence of higher-order interactions, a multicritical point exists where the states with all possible values of composite spin are degenerate, leading to maximal ground state degeneracy. The case $s=σ=1$ with bilinear and biquadratic interactions is studied in detail. Its phase diagram comprises three phases - F, Ferri and MD, which meet at a triple point. On the phase boundaries, the ground state becomes macroscopically degenerate. For the diamond chain, we calculate the ground state degeneracy exactly; for higher dimensions, the problem maps onto a bond percolation framework, solved numerically. The residual entropy per spin reaches up to $60\%$ of the maximal value, peaking at the triple point. Low-temperature magnetization curves in external magnetic fields exhibit plateaus and jumps. The excitation spectrum is gapped in the MD phase, gapless in the F phase, and resembles that of the Lieb-Mattis ferrimagnet in the Ferri phase. The high residual entropy suggests potential applications in ultra-low-temperature cooling and quantum thermal machines.
summary: This theoretical work analyzes a complex spin model on diamond-decorated lattices exhibiting competing magnetic interactions. By exploiting local spin conservation, the authors map out a phase diagram featuring three distinct magnetic phases. The key findings highlight that the system achieves maximal ground state degeneracy and high residual entropy at a multicritical point, suggesting applications in quantum cooling technologies.
main_problem: The paper investigates the ground state properties, magnetization, and low-temperature thermodynamics of a spin-$s$ model with competing ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic interactions on diamond-decorated lattices.
main_result: The system exhibits rich phase diagrams (F, Ferri, MD) meeting at a multicritical point where the ground state degeneracy is maximal, leading to high residual entropy.
method: The analysis combines exact diagonalization for the 1D chain with mapping the 2D/3D problem onto a bond percolation framework for calculating ground state degeneracy and magnetization.
model_or_system: The model is a spin-$s$ Heisenberg model defined on diamond-decorated lattices, incorporating bilinear and higher-order exchange interactions. Local conservation of the composite spin on each diamond diagonal allows for the exact analysis of local energy minimization.
key_observables: Ground state degeneracy ($W$), residual entropy per spin ($S_0$), low-temperature magnetization ($m$), and the excitation spectrum (gapped/gapless).
important_parameters: The spin values ($s, \sigma$), the coupling ratios ($J, K$), and the lattice dimensionality (1D chain, 2D, 3D).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the local conservation of the composite spin on each diamond unit, and for higher dimensions, the problem is mapped onto a bond percolation framework.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the lattice structure and couplings (Fig. 1, 2). Phase diagrams show the three competing phases (F, Ferri, MD) meeting at a triple point (Fig. 3). Further figures detail magnetization curves and percolation exponents (Fig. 4, 5, 6).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the general formalism using local spin conservation. It then analyzes the pure Heisenberg case, detailing $2\sigma$ transitions. The focus shifts to the $s=\sigma=1$ case, mapping the phase diagram and calculating degeneracy/entropy. Finally, it analyzes magnetization and specific heat across different lattice dimensions and critical lines.
why_interesting: The study of high residual entropy and macroscopic degeneracy at multicritical points is highly relevant to understanding quantum phase transitions and the potential for quantum thermal engines.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
